translatedContent: I was tired of the limitations of AceStream and some of the problems that

originContent: I found quite frustrating the experience of using AceStream in a home network
translatedContent: I found quite frustrating the experience of using AceStream in a home network

originContent: with a single server and multiple clients, to try to optimize resources. This
translatedContent: with a single server and multiple clients, to try to optimize resources. This

originContent: is the topology for which I am using AceStream:
translatedContent: is the topology for which I am using AceStream:

originContent: * Only **one client** can play the same stream at a time 🚫.
translatedContent: * Only **one client** can play the same stream at a time 🚫.

originContent: * Having each client to run AceStream on their own is a waste of resources
translatedContent: * Having each client to run AceStream on their own is a waste of resources

originContent: * Multiple clients can play different streams if they have a unique `pid`
translatedContent: * Multiple clients can play different streams if they have a unique `pid`

originContent: * The standard AceStream HTTP API is not resilient enough against errors,
translatedContent: * The standard AceStream HTTP API is not resilient enough against errors,

originContent: if the transmission stops it stops for every client ❌.
translatedContent: if the transmission stops it stops for every client ❌.

originContent: * A single, centralized server running **all your AceStream streams** ⛓.
translatedContent: * A single, centralized server running **all your AceStream streams** ⛓.

originContent: * Automatic assignation of a unique `pid` (Player ID) **per client per stream** 🪪.
translatedContent: * Automatic assignation of a unique `pid` (Player ID) **per client per stream** 🪪.

originContent: * **Stream Multiplexing** 🕎: The same stream can be reproduced *at the
translatedContent: * **Stream Multiplexing** 🕎: The same stream can be reproduced *at the

originContent: * **Resilient, error-proof** streaming thanks to the HTTP Middleware 🛡.
translatedContent: * **Resilient, error-proof** streaming thanks to the HTTP Middleware 🛡.

originContent: * *Blazing fast, minimal proxy* ☄ written in Go!
translatedContent: * *Blazing fast, minimal proxy* ☄ written in Go!

AceStream underneath attempts to use UPnP IGD to connect against a remote machine.
translatedContent: AceStream underneath attempts to use UPnP IGD to connect against a remote machine.

originContent: The problem is that this is not working because of the bridging layer added by Docker
translatedContent: The problem is that this is not working because of the bridging layer added by Docker

originContent: If you are running a single instance of Acexy - and a single instance of AceStream -
translatedContent: If you are running a single instance of Acexy - and a single instance of AceStream -

originContent: it should be safe for you to run the container with *host networking*. This means:
translatedContent: it should be safe for you to run the container with *host networking*. This means:

originContent: - The container **can access** any other application bridged to your main network.
translatedContent: - The container **can access** any other application bridged to your main network.

originContent: - You **don't need** to expose any ports.
translatedContent: - You **don't need** to expose any ports.

originContent: > NOTE: This only works on Linux environments. See https://docs.docker.com/engine/network/drivers/host/
translatedContent: > NOTE: This only works on Linux environments. See https://docs.docker.com/engine/network/drivers/host/

originContent: That should enable AceStream to use UPnP freely.
translatedContent: That should enable AceStream to use UPnP freely.
